key_verses.title: Free from Expectation
key_verses.sanskrit:
अनपेक्षः(श्) शुचिर्दक्ष(:) उदासीनो गतव्यथः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः(स्) स मे प्रियः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
anapekṣaḥ(sh) śucirdakṣa(ḥ) udāsīno gatavyathaḥ ।
sarvārambhaparityāgī yo madbhaktaḥ(s) sa me priyaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the devotee who is free from dependence and expectation, pure, efficient, impartial, free from sorrow, and who has renounced all selfish undertakings, is dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta. This verse focuses on the inner freedom and practical maturity of such a devotee.
The first word is anapekṣaḥ.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta. This verse focuses on the inner freedom and practical maturity of such a devotee.
The first word is anapekṣaḥ. This means one who is not emotionally dependent on external factors for happiness. Dependence expresses itself as expectation. When we depend on a thing, person, situation, result, or behavior, we naturally expect it to be a certain way. When that expectation is not fulfilled, disappointment and frustration arise. The parā-bhakta is free from this dependence, and therefore free from binding expectation.
Then comes śuciḥ — pure. This purity is not merely physical cleanliness, though external cleanliness has its place. It also includes purity of motive, purity of conduct, and purity of mind. The devotee’s inner life is not driven by manipulation, hidden selfishness, or crookedness.
The devotee is also dakṣaḥ — efficient, alert, capable, and resourceful. Spirituality is not carelessness or incompetence. A mature devotee is able to do what has to be done properly, intelligently, and at the right time.
Udāsīnaḥ means impartial. The devotee is not swept away by personal likes and dislikes. He can stand apart inwardly and respond with fairness. Gatavyathaḥ means one from whom mental pain or sorrow has gone. Since expectations and dependence have been loosened, the mind is not constantly wounded by life.
Finally, sarvārambha-parityāgī means one who has given up all selfish undertakings. This does not mean giving up all action. It means giving up ego-driven projects born of insecurity, ambition, possessiveness, or self-importance. Such a devotee may still act, serve, teach, guide, and work, but not from personal craving. Such a devotee is dear to Bhagavān.
key_verses.story:
In the life of Śrī Candraśekharendra Sarasvatī, the sage of Kāñcī, we see a beautiful example of simplicity, purity, alertness, and freedom from expectation.
He lived with almost nothing. Though thousands revered him, he did not live like someone who depended on honor, comfort, possessions, or recognition. His food was simple. His movements were simple. His personal needs were few.
People from many backgrounds came to him — scholars, villagers, politicians, children, devotees, skeptics, and the distressed. He listened with attention. He did not respond from personal preference. Whether a person was rich or poor, famous or unknown, learned or simple, he gave attention according to what was needed.
There was also great dakṣatā in him. People sometimes imagine that a renunciate is detached because he is inactive or indifferent. But he was highly alert. He remembered details, guided institutions, protected traditions, encouraged learning, advised people in personal and spiritual matters, and acted with remarkable practical intelligence.
At the same time, he did not appear to carry personal anxiety. Things came; things went. Praise came; criticism came. Crowds came; solitude came. He remained inwardly light.
His life shows anapekṣatvam: freedom from dependence and expectation. It shows śaucam: purity and simplicity. It shows dakṣatā: alert competence. It shows udāsīnatā: impartiality. And it shows sarvārambha-parityāga: no selfish project, no personal empire-building, no ego-driven agenda.
He acted, but he did not act for himself.
key_verses.real_life_example:
A large part of modern frustration comes from expectation. We expect children to behave a certain way, colleagues to appreciate us, friends to respond immediately, institutions to be fair, traffic to move quickly, and life to follow our plan. When these expectations fail, the mind becomes restless.
This verse does not ask us to become careless or passive. It asks us to notice emotional dependence. We can care without leaning. We can love without controlling. We can work without expecting the world to obey our script.
The word dakṣaḥ is also very relevant. Spiritual life is sometimes misunderstood as becoming vague or impractical. But Kṛṣṇa praises efficiency. A devotee should be alert, responsible, and capable.
The verse also helps in leadership and family life. Impartiality is needed whenever we deal with people. If we act only from likes and dislikes, we become unfair. If we are inwardly free, we can respond with clarity.
Finally, sarvārambha-parityāga asks us to examine our projects. Are we acting for dharma, service, and responsibility? Or are we driven by ego, insecurity, comparison, and the need to prove ourselves?
key_verses.reflection:
Where do my expectations create repeated disappointment and frustration?
Are my actions guided by clarity and duty, or by ego-driven self-importance?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose you help your friend, but you expect your friend to praise you every time. If your friend forgets, you feel sad or angry.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that a good devotee does good things without always expecting something back.
A good devotee is also clean, careful, fair, and calm. Such a person does what is right, but does not keep saying, ‘What will I get?’”
kids_content.story:
A child helped clean the classroom.
The child arranged books, picked up pencils, and wiped the board.
At first, the child kept looking at the teacher, hoping the teacher would say, “Very good!”
But the teacher was busy and did not notice immediately.
The child felt a little upset.
Then another child said, “The classroom looks so nice now. Everyone can use it.”
The child smiled and thought, “I helped because it was good to help.”
Later the teacher noticed and thanked the child. But by then, the child had already felt happy inside.
That is what Kṛṣṇa is teaching — do the right thing carefully, without depending on praise.
kids_content.reflection:
Can you help someone without waiting for praise?
Can you do your work neatly and carefully because it is the right thing to do?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Freedom from Dependence
Phrase: anapekṣaḥ = free from expectation / dependence
The devotee is not emotionally dependent on external factors for happiness. Dependence produces expectation; expectation produces disappointment. Freedom from dependence gives freedom from frustration.
Step 2: Purity
Phrase: śuciḥ = pure
Purity includes external cleanliness, but more importantly purity of mind, motive, speech, and conduct. The devotee is not inwardly crooked or manipulative.
Step 3: Efficiency
Phrase: dakṣaḥ = efficient, capable, alert
The devotee is not careless. Spiritual maturity includes practical alertness and the capacity to do what has to be done properly.
Step 4: Impartiality
Phrase: udāsīnaḥ = impartial / inwardly uninvolved
The devotee is not carried away by likes and dislikes. He can stand apart inwardly and respond fairly.
Step 5: Freedom from Sorrow
Phrase: gatavyathaḥ = free from mental pain / sorrow
Since the devotee does not depend on external factors and does not live with binding expectations, the mind is not repeatedly wounded by disappointment.
Step 6: Renunciation of Selfish Undertakings
Phrase: sarvārambhaparityāgī = one who has renounced all selfish undertakings
This does not mean abandoning all action. It means giving up ego-driven, selfish, anxiety-born projects. The devotee may act vigorously, but not from personal craving or self-importance.
Step 7: Dear to Bhagavān
Phrase: yo madbhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ = that devotee of Mine is dear to Me
The one who has these qualities is a true devotee. Such a person is dear to Bhagavān because the mind is free, pure, capable, impartial, and no longer driven by selfish beginnings.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Śrī Nārāyaṇa Guru and the Temple Consecration
In Kerala, there was a time when many people were denied dignity and access to worship because of birth-based social divisions.
Śrī Nārāyaṇa Guru saw this suffering clearly. But he did not respond with hatred. He did not begin an ego-driven movement to defeat one group and glorify another. His response came from purity, clarity, and concern for upliftment.
At Aruvippuram, he consecrated a Śiva liṅga.
This was a powerful act. Many people questioned him, because they believed only certain people had the right to perform such a consecration.
When challenged, Śrī Nārāyaṇa Guru is remembered for responding with calm intelligence and spiritual clarity. He did not become agitated. He did not speak with bitterness. He showed that worship of Bhagavān cannot be confined by narrow social arrogance.
His action was dakṣatā — skillful, alert, and timely. It was also śuciḥ — pure in motive. He did not act for personal fame or anger, but for dharma, dignity, and spiritual access.
This reflects sarvārambha-parityāga. Though he undertook great work, it was not a selfish undertaking. It was action free from egoistic ambition.
2. Story of Swāmī Tapovanam and the Student in the Himalayas
Swāmī Tapovanam lived in the Himalayas with great simplicity. The cold was intense, the surroundings were austere, and there were very few comforts.
One day, a student approached him, hoping to study Vedānta. The student was inspired, but not yet prepared for the discipline required. Life in the Himalayas was not easy. Food was simple. Walking was difficult. The climate was harsh. There was no room for luxury or complaint.
Swāmī Tapovanam did not entertain emotional dependence. He did not soften the path merely to satisfy the student’s comfort. Nor did he reject the student harshly.
He taught with clarity and expected discipline.
The student gradually saw that spiritual life was not about dramatic emotion or outer appearance. It required purity, steadiness, and freedom from unnecessary dependence.
Swāmī Tapovanam’s life itself was the teaching. He did not need many possessions. He did not launch restless projects. He did not depend on crowds or praise. When students came, he taught. When solitude came, he remained content.
This shows anapekṣaḥ and sarvārambha-parityāgī. He was free from expectation, yet not inactive. He acted only as required by dharma and teaching.
3. Story of Śrī Śāradā Devī Cooking for Devotees
Śrī Śāradā Devī lived with extraordinary simplicity. Many devotees came to her — some peaceful, some troubled, some immature, some deeply wounded by life.
One day, after a long and tiring day, people still came needing food, attention, and comfort. In an ordinary mind, expectation would arise: “Why do people keep coming? Why should I serve again? Why is there no rest?”
But Śrī Śāradā Devī did not relate to service in that way.
She cooked, served, listened, and cared with motherly patience. Her work was not dramatic, but it was pure. There was no demand for recognition. There was no complaint that people did not understand her burden.
Her purity was not merely ritual purity. It was purity of heart.
She did what had to be done, quietly and carefully. She was not careless. She was not sentimental in a weak way. She was practical, attentive, and inwardly free.
This reflects śuciḥ, dakṣaḥ, and gatavyathaḥ. There was purity, efficiency, and freedom from inner complaint.
4. Story of Swāmī Śivānanda and the Sick Visitor
At the āśrama in Ṛṣikesh, a sick and tired visitor once arrived needing help. In many places, such a person might have been seen as an inconvenience.
But Swāmī Śivānanda had the eye of service.
He noticed the person’s condition and responded immediately. Food, medicine, rest, and care were arranged. He did not merely give a lecture on spirituality while ignoring practical suffering. His spirituality expressed itself as alert action.
This was dakṣatā — practical efficiency.
At the same time, there was no sense of personal display. He did not act so that people would praise him as compassionate. He did not serve with the expectation that the visitor should become loyal to him. Service was simply what the situation required.
If someone praised him, he moved on. If someone criticized, he moved on. The work continued.
This shows anapekṣaḥ and dakṣaḥ together. Freedom from expectation did not make him dull. It made his action cleaner, quicker, and more useful.
5. Story of Ānandamayī Mā and the Grieving Devotee
A grieving devotee once came to Ānandamayī Mā, overwhelmed by personal sorrow. The person was not ready for philosophy. Words alone would not help.
Ānandamayī Mā received the devotee with quiet presence.
She did not become agitated by the grief. She did not push it away. She did not turn it into a personal burden. She allowed the person to sit, weep, and slowly become quiet in her presence.
There was deep compassion, but not emotional disturbance.
This is a subtle quality. Many people either become indifferent to another’s pain, or they get swallowed by it. Ānandamayī Mā showed another way — caring without losing inner steadiness.
Her presence gave comfort because she was not inwardly shaken. She was available, but not dependent. Loving, but not overwhelmed. Responsive, but not restless.
This reflects udāsīnaḥ and gatavyathaḥ. Here udāsīnaḥ does not mean cold indifference. It means inner poise, impartiality, and freedom from being dragged into disturbance.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
अनपेक्षः शुचिः दक्षः उदासीनः गतव्यथः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यः मद्भक्तः सः मे प्रियः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: अनपेक्षः = free from expectation / independent | शुचिः = pure | दक्षः = efficient / capable | उदासीनः = impartial / indifferent in a healthy way | गतव्यथः = free from sorrow / mental pain | सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी = one who has renounced all selfish undertakings | यः = who | मद्भक्तः = My devotee | सः = he | मे = to Me / for Me | प्रियः = dear
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः अनपेक्षः, शुचिः, दक्षः, उदासीनः, गतव्यथः, सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी, मद्भक्तः, सः मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: शुचिः + दक्षः = शुचिर्दक्षः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga after इ changes to र् before the following soft consonant द्. | दक्षः + उदासीनः = दक्ष उदासीनः | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः / विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga is dropped before the following vowel उ. | सः + मे = स मे | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः; final visarga is dropped before the following consonant म्. | मत् + भक्तः = मद्भक्तः | Sandhi name: व्यञ्जनसन्धिः / जश्त्वसन्धिः; final त् becomes द् before the voiced consonant भ्.
rich_grammar.samasa: गतव्यथः = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: गता व्यथा यस्य सः — one whose sorrow has gone. | सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: सर्वेषाम् आरम्भाणाम् परित्यागः यस्य सः — one who has renounced all undertakings, especially selfish or ego-driven undertakings. | मद्भक्तः = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष समासः | Breakdown: मम भक्तः — My devotee. | अनपेक्षः = नञ्-तत्पुरुष sense | Breakdown: न अपेक्षा यस्य / अपेक्षारहितः — one free from expectation.
rich_grammar.other: गत in गतव्यथः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from गम् धातुः, meaning “gone.” | भक्तः in मद्भक्तः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त form from भज् धातुः. | परित्यागी is formed with the त्यागिन् sense, meaning “one who renounces.” | All the descriptive terms are प्रथमा-एकवचनम् and qualify the implied devotee. | प्रियः is also प्रथमा-एकवचनम् and completes the statement “he is dear.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: Freedom from dependence and expectation, purity, efficiency, impartiality, and renunciation of selfish projects mark the mature devotee. Spiritual life is not carelessness but alert, capable living without ego-driven ambition.

key_verses.title: Beyond Elation and Grief
key_verses.sanskrit:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः(स्) स मे प्रियः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
yo na hṛṣyati na dveṣṭi na śocati na kāṅkṣati ।
śubhāśubhaparityāgī bhaktimānyaḥ(s) sa me priyaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the devotee who is not overpowered by elation, hatred, grief, or craving, and who gives up both puṇya and pāpa as binding pursuits, is dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gained inner strength through knowledge and spiritual maturity. This verse focuses on freedom from emotional extremes.
The words na hṛṣyati and na śocati should be understood together.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gained inner strength through knowledge and spiritual maturity. This verse focuses on freedom from emotional extremes.
The words na hṛṣyati and na śocati should be understood together. The devotee is not overpowered by elation when favorable situations come, and is not overpowered by depression when unfavorable situations come. Happiness and disturbance may naturally arise, but they do not become so intense that they cloud viveka, the discriminative faculty.
Similarly, na dveṣṭi and na kāṅkṣati go together. The devotee is free from binding dveṣa and binding rāga. Dveṣa is strong aversion, the tendency to reject, hate, or resist what is unpleasant. Kāṅkṣā is craving, the mind’s insistence that something must come for me to be complete. The parā-bhakta may function in the world, but is not inwardly ruled by these two forces.
The phrase śubhāśubhaparityāgī means one who has given up both auspicious and inauspicious as binding pursuits. This does not mean such a person avoids noble action. Noble actions may still be done, but not for the sake of collecting puṇya or gaining a better future experience. Puṇya and pāpa both keep one connected to saṁsāra. Puṇya is like a smiling binding force; pāpa is like a frowning binding force. The mature devotee does not act for either. Noble actions are done for citta-śuddhi, jñāna-niṣṭhā, dharma, or loka-saṅgraha, not for personal karmic acquisition.
Such a devotee is bhaktimān — endowed with mature devotion. This devotion is not emotional dependence. It is the devotion of one whose mind has become steady, whose reactions do not overpower viveka, and whose actions are free from binding self-interest. Such a devotee is dear to Bhagavān.
key_verses.story:
In the Rāmāyaṇa, when the news came that Rāma must go to the forest, Ayodhyā was shaken.
The coronation had been prepared. The city was ready to celebrate. Rāma was to be crowned, and everyone expected joy, honor, and prosperity.
Then everything changed.
Because of Kaikeyī’s boons, Daśaratha had to send Rāma to the forest for fourteen years. The news was unbearable to many, especially Lakṣmaṇa.
Lakṣmaṇa’s love for Rāma was intense. When he heard what had happened, anger rose like fire. He could not tolerate what he saw as injustice. He spoke harshly about Daśaratha and Kaikeyī. In the heat of emotion, his discrimination became clouded. He even spoke of fighting against the decision.
This is what happens when emotional upheaval becomes too intense. Love was there. Dharma was there. But anger began to cover viveka.
Rāma, however, stood differently.
He did not rejoice when coronation was announced, and he did not collapse when exile was declared. He did not hate Kaikeyī. He did not grieve in a way that destroyed his clarity. He did not crave the kingdom that was taken away.
He calmly accepted the situation as dharma.
Then he gently cooled Lakṣmaṇa’s mind. He helped him see that action must not come from rage. Even in pain, the intellect must remain clear.
This story beautifully illustrates na hṛṣyati, na dveṣṭi, na śocati, and na kāṅkṣati. Emotional reactions may arise, but the parā-bhakta does not allow them to overpower viveka.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Modern life constantly pulls us into emotional extremes. A promotion comes, and we become over-excited. A criticism comes, and we feel crushed. Someone succeeds, and envy appears. A plan fails, and grief or anger takes over.
This verse does not say we should become emotionless. It says we should not become overpowered. There is a difference between feeling an emotion and being ruled by it.
The most practical teaching here is to preserve inner space. When anger, excitement, grief, or craving arises, we should be able to notice: “My mind is becoming disturbed. My discrimination may be getting clouded.” That much awareness allows us to postpone harmful speech and action.
This is especially important in family life, workplace conflicts, emails, messages, and social media. Many mistakes happen because we respond while emotionally clouded. If we pause, the same situation can be handled with greater clarity.
The verse also challenges our obsession with results. Even good actions can become binding if they are done only to collect puṇya, recognition, superiority, or future reward. The mature way is to do what is right because it is right, not because we want to emotionally possess the result.
key_verses.reflection:
When strong emotion comes, do I still have enough inner space to protect my viveka?
Do I do good actions for inner maturity and dharma, or mainly to gain recognition, puṇya, or reward?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Sometimes good things happen and we become very excited. Sometimes bad things happen and we become very sad or angry.
Kṛṣṇa is not saying, ‘Do not feel anything.’ He is saying, ‘Do not let feelings take control of you.’
If you are very angry, wait before speaking. If you are very excited, still think clearly. A good devotee keeps the mind steady.”
kids_content.story:
A child was playing a board game.
At first, the child was winning and became very excited. The child laughed loudly and teased the others.
Then suddenly the game changed, and the child started losing.
Now the child became angry and wanted to throw the pieces.
The parent said, “Pause. Winning and losing are both part of the game. Can you enjoy the game without becoming too excited or too upset?”
The child took a deep breath.
The game continued. This time, the child played more calmly.
Kṛṣṇa is teaching us the same thing: enjoy what comes, face what comes, but do not let the mind lose balance.
kids_content.reflection:
What do you do when you feel very angry or very excited?
Can you pause before speaking when your feelings are very strong?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Freedom from Overpowering Elation
Phrase: yo na hṛṣyati = one who does not become over-elated
The devotee is not carried away by extreme excitement when favorable situations come. Happiness may arise, but it does not become so intense that it clouds discrimination.
Step 2: Freedom from Hatred
Phrase: na dveṣṭi = does not hate / does not fall into aversion
The devotee is free from binding dveṣa. Unpleasant situations may come, but the mind does not convert them into hatred, rejection, or revengeful resistance.
Step 3: Freedom from Overpowering Grief
Phrase: na śocati = does not grieve
This does not mean there is no human sadness. It means the devotee is not overpowered by depression or sorrow. Emotional pain does not destroy viveka.
Step 4: Freedom from Craving
Phrase: na kāṅkṣati = does not crave / does not long for acquisition
The devotee is not ruled by rāga. The mind does not insist that a particular object, person, outcome, or situation is necessary for completion.
Step 5: The Pairs to Be Read Together
Phrase: na hṛṣyati … na śocati and na dveṣṭi … na kāṅkṣati = neither elation nor depression; neither aversion nor craving
The first pair refers to emotional reactions to favorable and unfavorable situations. The second pair refers to the underlying forces of rāga and dveṣa. The teaching is not emotional suppression, but emotional mastery.
Step 6: Renunciation of Good and Evil as Binding Pursuits
Phrase: śubhāśubhaparityāgī = one who has renounced both auspicious and inauspicious
The devotee gives up both pāpa and puṇya as binding pursuits. Pāpa obviously binds, but puṇya also binds when pursued for personal karmic enjoyment. Noble actions may still be performed, but not for egoistic acquisition of puṇya.
Step 7: Mature Devotion
Phrase: bhaktimānyaḥ sa me priyaḥ = such a devoted one is dear to Me
This is not ordinary emotional devotion. This is mature devotion in which the mind is steady, viveka is protected, rāga-dveṣa are weakened, and action is free from binding pursuit of karmic results.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Yudhiṣṭhira at the Yakṣa Lake
During the forest exile, the Pāṇḍavas once became terribly thirsty. One by one, the brothers went in search of water and reached a beautiful lake.
But a mysterious voice warned them: “Do not drink before answering my questions.”
Driven by thirst, the brothers ignored the warning and fell lifeless near the lake.
Finally Yudhiṣṭhira arrived.
He saw his brothers lying still. The sight was unbearable. These were not strangers; they were his own brothers, his strength, his companions in exile. Grief could have overwhelmed him. Anger could have risen. Desperation could have made him rush to drink.
But Yudhiṣṭhira paused.
He heard the Yakṣa’s warning and did not allow sorrow to cloud his judgment. He answered question after question with clarity. His viveka remained available even in crisis.
At the end, the Yakṣa was pleased and asked him to choose one brother to be restored. Yudhiṣṭhira did not choose selfishly. He chose Nakula, so that both Kuntī and Mādrī would each have one living son.
This story shows the spirit of 12.17. A mature mind may feel pain, but it does not become overpowered by pain. It preserves discrimination even when the situation is emotionally intense.
2. Story of Ruru and the Serpent
In the Mahābhārata, Ruru loved Pramadvarā deeply. Their marriage was arranged, and joy filled his heart.
Then tragedy struck.
Pramadvarā was bitten by a snake and fell lifeless. Ruru was shattered. Later, through divine arrangement, she was restored to life, but the shock left a deep mark on him.
His grief turned into hatred. He made a vow to kill snakes wherever he found them.
One day, Ruru saw an old harmless serpent and raised his weapon to kill it.
The serpent spoke.
It explained that not all serpents were guilty. Why should an innocent being be destroyed because of Ruru’s hatred toward another? The serpent’s words made him reflect.
Ruru realized that grief had become dveṣa. His pain had made him unjust.
He stopped.
This story shows how sorrow can turn into hatred when viveka is lost. It also shows the beginning of mastery: the moment we recognize that emotion is clouding judgment, we can step back.
3. Story of King Parīkṣit Hearing the Bhāgavata
King Parīkṣit once made a grave mistake. Tired, thirsty, and disturbed, he insulted a sage by placing a dead snake around his neck. The sage’s son cursed him: in seven days, he would die by snakebite.
When Parīkṣit heard the curse, he could have reacted in many ways. He could have become furious. He could have tried to punish the boy. He could have clung desperately to the kingdom. He could have drowned in fear.
Instead, he recognized the situation as a call to turn fully toward the highest truth.
He gave up attachment to the throne, sat on the banks of Gaṅgā, and listened to Śuka teach the Bhāgavata.
The remaining days of life became sacred.
This story reflects na śocati and na kāṅkṣati. Parīkṣit did not waste the little time left in grief or craving. He turned toward Bhagavān and used the crisis for liberation.
4. Story of Yayāti Returning the Borrowed Youth
King Yayāti was cursed with old age while still full of desire. Unable to bear the loss of youth, he asked his sons if one of them would exchange youth for his old age.
Pūru agreed.
With renewed youth, Yayāti enjoyed pleasures for a long time. He had power, opportunity, and the means to fulfill desire. But after long enjoyment, a realization arose: desire is not ended by feeding it.
He understood that craving does not become quiet by indulgence. Like fire fed with ghee, it only grows.
Finally, Yayāti returned the youth to Pūru and accepted old age. More importantly, he turned away from the illusion that enjoyment could complete him.
This story illustrates na kāṅkṣati. The mature mind sees the limitation of craving. It does not keep chasing objects in the hope of becoming complete.
5. Story of Khaṭvāṅga Giving Up Heavenly Reward
King Khaṭvāṅga once helped the devas in battle. Pleased with his service, they offered him a boon.
He asked how much time remained in his life.
The answer was startling: only a very short time.
In that moment, Khaṭvāṅga did not ask for heaven, pleasure, fame, kingdom, or extended enjoyment. He saw the urgency of truth.
He returned to earth, withdrew the mind from worldly pursuits, and turned it completely toward Bhagavān.
This story reflects śubhāśubhaparityāgī. Even heavenly reward, even puṇya-phala, is not the goal for the mature seeker. When the highest truth is seen as the goal, both pāpa and puṇya are no longer pursued as ends.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
यः न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान् यः सः मे प्रियः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: यः = who | न = not | हृष्यति = rejoices / becomes elated | न = not | द्वेष्टि = hates / has aversion | न = not | शोचति = grieves | न = not | काङ्क्षति = desires / craves | शुभाशुभपरित्यागी = one who has renounced auspicious and inauspicious | भक्तिमान् = endowed with devotion | यः = who | सः = he | मे = to Me / for Me | प्रियः = dear
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः न हृष्यति, न द्वेष्टि, न शोचति, न काङ्क्षति, यः शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्, सः मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: शुभ + अशुभ = शुभाशुभ | Sandhi name: सवर्णदीर्घसन्धिः; अ + अ becomes आ. | भक्तिमान् + यः = भक्तिमान्यः | Sandhi name: संयोगसन्धिः; final न् joins with य् to form न्य in continuous pronunciation. | सः + मे = स मे | Sandhi name: विसर्गलोपः; final visarga is dropped before the following consonant म्.
rich_grammar.samasa: शुभाशुभ = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: शुभम् च अशुभम् च — auspicious and inauspicious. | शुभाशुभपरित्यागी = षष्ठी तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: शुभस्य अशुभस्य च परित्यागः यस्य सः — one who has renounced both auspicious and inauspicious as binding pursuits.
rich_grammar.other: हृष्यति, द्वेष्टि, शोचति, and काङ्क्षति are all लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुषः, एकवचनम् forms. | भक्तिमान् is formed with मतुप्-प्रत्ययः, meaning “one who possesses bhakti.” | परित्यागी has the त्यागिन् sense, meaning “one who renounces.” | The repeated न emphasizes freedom from the four reactions: elation, hatred, grief, and craving. | The first यः and the later यः both refer to the same implied devotee; सः मे प्रियः completes the sentence.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The parā-bhakta is not overpowered by elation, hatred, grief, or craving, and does not pursue either puṇya or pāpa as binding goals. True devotion preserves inner discrimination through all emotional weather.

key_verses.title: Equal in All Conditions
key_verses.sanskrit:
समः(श्) शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः(स्) सङ्गविवर्जितः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
samaḥ(sh) śatrau ca mitre ca tathā mānāpamānayoḥ ।
śītoṣṇasukhaduḥkheṣu samaḥ(s) saṅgavivarjitaḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the mature devotee is the same toward enemy and friend, honor and dishonor, heat and cold, pleasure and pain, and is free from attachment.
key_verses.concise_journey:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gone through the disciplines of bhakti-yoga and has gained inner steadiness. In this verse, the central word is samaḥ — the one who remains the same.
First, the devotee is samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca — the same toward enemy and friend.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gone through the disciplines of bhakti-yoga and has gained inner steadiness. In this verse, the central word is samaḥ — the one who remains the same.
First, the devotee is samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca — the same toward enemy and friend. This does not mean a friend and an enemy are treated identically in practical life. A friend may be welcomed; an enemy may require caution. But inwardly, the devotee is not ruled by attachment toward the friend or hatred toward the enemy. The response is governed by dharma and clarity, not by emotional partiality.
Then Bhagavān says the devotee is the same in māna and apamāna — honor and dishonor. Praise, respect, recognition, insult, neglect, and humiliation do not become the basis of the person’s self-worth. The mature devotee may receive honor without inflation and dishonor without inner collapse.
The same steadiness applies to śīta-uṣṇa and sukha-duḥkha — heat and cold, pleasure and pain. Physical and emotional experiences continue to come and go, but the devotee is not internally enslaved by them.
The verse ends with saṅga-vivarjitaḥ — free from attachment. This is the foundation of the previous qualities. Attachment creates dependence; dependence creates fear, partiality, and agitation. When attachment is loosened, the mind becomes available for sameness.
key_verses.story:
The life of Haridāsa Ṭhākura gives a powerful example of this verse.
Haridāsa Ṭhākura was deeply devoted to nāma-saṅkīrtana, the repetition and singing of Bhagavān’s name. His life was centered on remembrance of Bhagavān, not on social approval.
But not everyone honored him. Some people opposed him because of religious and social prejudice. His devotion was misunderstood, and he was punished severely.
In one famous incident, he was ordered to be beaten in public marketplaces.
The punishment was brutal. The people who struck him were not friends. They were acting as enemies. The body suffered. The situation was humiliating. Onlookers watched. The world could easily have become divided in his mind: “These are my enemies; these are my supporters; this is honor; this is dishonor; this is pain; this is injustice.”
But Haridāsa Ṭhākura’s mind did not turn to hatred.
As he was beaten, he continued remembering Bhagavān. More than that, he prayed for those who were hurting him. He did not want them to suffer the consequences of their action.
This is not ordinary tolerance. It is deep inner freedom.
Pain was there, but hatred did not arise. Dishonor was there, but his dignity did not depend on public respect. Enemies were there, but his heart did not become poisoned.
This story beautifully reflects samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca, mānāpamānayoḥ samaḥ, and sukha-duḥkheṣu samaḥ. The mature devotee is not made or broken by how the world behaves.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Modern life constantly divides people into “my side” and “their side.” We do this in families, workplaces, politics, religion, online discussions, and even spiritual communities. Once someone becomes “enemy” in our mind, we easily justify hatred. Once someone becomes “my person,” we may overlook wrong behavior.
This verse asks for maturity. We can recognize difference in behavior without hatred in the heart. We can love a friend without blind attachment. We can respond to an opponent without inner poison.
Honor and dishonor are also powerful today. A small praise can make us feel inflated; a small criticism can disturb us for days. Social media has made this worse. Likes, comments, recognition, and public image easily become emotional food.
The teaching is not to become dull or indifferent. It is to stop outsourcing our inner balance to the world. Weather will change, people will change, comfort will come and go, praise and blame will alternate. The mature mind remains anchored.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I treat people according to dharma, or according to attachment and dislike?
How much does my peace depend on praise, comfort, recognition, and approval?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Sometimes someone is nice to us, and we call them our friend. Sometimes someone is not nice, and we may feel they are against us.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that a good devotee should not become full of hatred toward one person or too attached to another. We should try to be fair and kind.
Also, if someone praises us, we should not become too proud. If someone criticizes us, we should not feel completely broken. We should stay steady inside.”
kids_content.story:
A child brought a drawing to school.
One friend said, “This is beautiful!”
The child felt very happy.
Then another child said, “I don’t like it.”
The child suddenly felt sad and wanted to tear the drawing.
The teacher gently said, “Pause. One person praised it. One person did not. But the drawing is still your work. Can you listen calmly and keep learning?”
The child looked again at the drawing.
The child smiled and said, “I can improve it, but I do not have to feel bad.”
That day, the child learned not to become too proud from praise or too sad from criticism.
kids_content.reflection:
What happens to your mind when someone praises you?
Can you stay calm when someone says something you do not like?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Sameness Toward Enemy and Friend
Phrase: samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca = the same toward enemy and friend
The mature devotee is not ruled by hatred toward an enemy or attachment toward a friend. Practical responses may differ, but the inner mind remains balanced.
Step 2: Sameness in Honor and Dishonor
Phrase: tathā mānāpamānayoḥ = likewise in honor and dishonor
The devotee is not inflated by honor and not shattered by dishonor. Respect and insult are received as passing experiences, not as definitions of the Self.
Step 3: Sameness in Heat and Cold
Phrase: śītoṣṇa = cold and heat
Physical conditions such as heat and cold continue to affect the body. But the mature mind does not allow bodily discomfort to dominate inner clarity.
Step 4: Sameness in Pleasure and Pain
Phrase: sukha-duḥkheṣu samaḥ = the same in pleasure and pain
Pleasure and pain arise in life, but the parā-bhakta remains steady. This does not mean the absence of sensation or feeling; it means freedom from inner slavery to them.
Step 5: Freedom from Attachment
Phrase: saṅga-vivarjitaḥ = free from attachment
Attachment is the root that makes the mind partial and disturbed. When attachment is loosened, the mind can remain balanced in friend and enemy, honor and dishonor, comfort and discomfort.
Step 6: The Teaching
Phrase: samaḥ … samaḥ saṅga-vivarjitaḥ = the same, the same, free from attachment
The repeated emphasis on sameness shows that the parā-bhakta’s stability is not situational. It is born of inner freedom. The world of pairs continues, but the devotee is not tossed about by the pairs.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Guru Nānak, Bhāī Lālo, and Malik Bhāgo
Guru Nānak once came to a town where two very different invitations awaited him.
Bhāī Lālo was a poor carpenter. His home was simple, and the food he could offer was plain. But it was earned honestly and offered with devotion.
Malik Bhāgo was wealthy and powerful. He arranged a grand feast and expected Guru Nānak to attend. There would be honor, display, and social prestige.
Guru Nānak chose to stay with Bhāī Lālo.
Malik Bhāgo felt insulted and questioned him. Why would a saint reject a rich man’s feast and accept the food of a poor carpenter?
Guru Nānak did not answer with anger. He showed the truth through an unforgettable act. Taking food from Bhāī Lālo and food from Malik Bhāgo, he pressed them. From Bhāī Lālo’s food came the sweetness of honest labor; from Malik Bhāgo’s food came the suffering caused by exploitation.
The point was not poverty versus wealth. The point was purity versus impurity.
Guru Nānak was not attracted by honor, nor afraid of displeasing power. He was not attached to comfort or status. He responded according to dharma.
This reflects mānāpamānayoḥ samaḥ and saṅga-vivarjitaḥ.
2. Story of Narsiṁha Mehtā and Social Humiliation
Narsiṁha Mehtā was a devotee of Kṛṣṇa whose heart overflowed in song and devotion. But society around him did not always honor him.
He moved freely among devotees, sang with love, and cared more for Bhagavān than for social approval. Because of this, some people mocked him and excluded him. They measured him by social standards; he measured life by devotion.
In one remembered incident, he was humiliated by people who doubted his worth and devotion. Instead of responding with anger or bitterness, he turned more deeply toward Kṛṣṇa.
His songs did not become songs of revenge. They became songs of surrender.
For Narsiṁha Mehtā, public honor was not the foundation of life. Dishonor too could not take away his inner wealth. His companionship was with Bhagavān.
This story reflects mānāpamānayoḥ samaḥ. The one who belongs inwardly to Bhagavān is not made big by honor or small by dishonor.
3. Story of Sītā in Aśoka-vana
In the Rāmāyaṇa, Sītā was held in Aśoka-vana in Laṅkā. She was surrounded by rākṣasīs, threatened by Rāvaṇa, and separated from Rāma.
The outer conditions were painful. There was no comfort, no royal honor, no familiar family, and no physical security. Rāvaṇa tried to tempt her with wealth, status, and queenly power. He also tried to frighten her.
But Sītā’s mind did not bend.
She did not hate in a way that made her lose dignity, and she did not crave the comforts being offered. She did not measure herself by the humiliation of captivity. Her heart remained steady in dharma and in devotion to Rāma.
Trijaṭā and others saw that Sītā’s strength was not outer strength. It was inner anchoring.
This reflects sukha-duḥkheṣu samaḥ and saṅga-vivarjitaḥ. Comfort and discomfort came; honor and dishonor came; threat and temptation came. But her inner commitment did not shift.
4. Story of Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār and the Temple Priest
Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār was a great devotee of Bhagavān Raṅganātha. Yet because of social prejudice, he was not allowed to enter the temple.
One day, while he was absorbed in devotion near the river, the temple priest Loka Sāranga came to collect water for worship. Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār, absorbed in Bhagavān, did not notice him immediately.
The priest became upset and threw a small stone to make him move.
Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār was hurt, but he did not respond with anger. He moved away quietly, feeling that he had obstructed service to Bhagavān.
Later, Bhagavān Raṅganātha appeared in the priest’s dream and revealed that the hurt given to the devotee had reached Bhagavān Himself. The priest was instructed to bring Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār into the temple on his shoulders.
The next day, the priest carried him into the sanctum. Tiruppāṇ Āḻvār beheld Bhagavān Raṅganātha and poured out the verses of the Amalan Ādhipirān.
This story shows sameness in dishonor and honor. When insulted, he did not hate. When honored and carried into the temple, he did not become proud. His mind remained only with Bhagavān.
5. Story of Lallā Ded Walking Through Praise and Blame
Lallā Ded of Kashmir lived as a mystic whose life did not fit ordinary social expectations.
Some people revered her as a saint. Others mocked her, misunderstood her, or judged her harshly. Praise and blame followed her as she moved through villages and fields.
But Lallā Ded did not build her identity on either.
She continued speaking from the depth of realization, using simple sayings that pointed people inward. Her life showed freedom from social approval and disapproval.
When people praised, she did not become inflated. When people blamed, she did not become broken. She had already turned away from dependence on outer labels.
This reflects mānāpamānayoḥ samaḥ and saṅga-vivarjitaḥ. The one who is free from attachment to image can move through praise and blame with inner steadiness.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: समः = equal / the same | शत्रौ = toward an enemy | च = and | मित्रे = toward a friend | च = and | तथा = likewise | मानापमानयोः = in honor and dishonor | शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु = in cold, heat, pleasure, and pain | समः = equal / the same | सङ्गविवर्जितः = free from attachment
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः शत्रौ च मित्रे च समः, तथा मानापमानयोः समः, शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः, सङ्गविवर्जितः, सः मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: मान + अपमानयोः = मानापमानयोः | Sandhi name: सवर्णदीर्घसन्धिः; final अ and initial अ combine into आ. | शीत + उष्ण = शीतोष्ण | Sandhi name: गुणसन्धिः; अ + उ becomes ओ. | सुख + दुःखेषु = सुखदुःखेषु | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final अ joins with द् without special phonetic change. | सङ्ग + विवर्जितः = सङ्गविवर्जितः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final अ joins with व् without special phonetic change.
rich_grammar.samasa: मानापमानयोः = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: मानः च अपमानः च — honor and dishonor. | शीतोष्ण = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: शीतम् च उष्णम् च — cold and heat. | सुखदुःखेषु = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: सुखम् च दुःखम् च — pleasure and pain. | शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु = द्वन्द्व समासः | Breakdown: शीतम् च उष्णम् च सुखम् च दुःखम् च — in cold, heat, pleasure, and pain. | सङ्गविवर्जितः = तृतीया तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: सङ्गेन विवर्जितः — one who is free from attachment.
rich_grammar.other: विवर्जितः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from वर्ज् धातुः with वि-उपसर्गः, meaning “devoid of” or “free from.” | शत्रौ and मित्रे are सप्तमी-एकवचनम् forms, indicating the field in which sameness is maintained. | मानापमानयोः is सप्तमी-द्विवचनम् because the pair honor-dishonor is treated as two. | शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु is सप्तमी-बहुवचनम् because multiple pairs are included. | समः appears twice for emphasis, showing sameness in both relational and experiential situations.

key_verses.final_takeaway: The mature devotee remains inwardly steady whether facing friend or enemy, honor or dishonor, pleasure or pain. This equanimity arises from freedom from attachment, not from indifference.

key_verses.title: Steady in Praise and Blame
key_verses.sanskrit:
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित् ।
अनिकेतः(स्) स्थिरमतिः(र्) भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
tulyanindāstutirmaunī santuṣṭo yena kenacit ।
aniketaḥ(s) sthiramatiḥ(r) bhaktimānme priyo naraḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa says that the devotee who is the same in criticism and praise, measured in speech, content with whatever comes, free from attachment to residence, and firm in Self-knowledge, is dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta. In this verse, the qualities are very practical and deeply inward.
The devotee is tulya-nindā-stutiḥ — the same in criticism and praise.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa continues describing the parā-bhakta. In this verse, the qualities are very practical and deeply inward.
The devotee is tulya-nindā-stutiḥ — the same in criticism and praise. Praise does not inflate the person, and criticism does not break the person. This does not mean praise and criticism are not heard. They may be heard, evaluated, and responded to if needed. But the person’s inner worth does not depend on public opinion.
The devotee is maunī. This does not necessarily mean one who has taken a vow of total silence. It means a person of measured, disciplined, limited speech. Such a devotee does not speak unnecessarily, impulsively, or hurtfully. Speech becomes thoughtful, useful, and restrained.
The devotee is santuṣṭaḥ yena kenacit — content with whatever comes. This does not mean carelessness or lack of effort. It means the person does not make inner peace depend on perfect external arrangements. Whatever comes by Bhagavān’s order is received with balance.
The devotee is aniketaḥ. Literally, this can mean “homeless,” but the deeper sense here is freedom from attachment to residence, possession, and fixed security. A person may live in a house, but inwardly should not be bound by the idea, “Only this place is my safety and identity.”
Finally, the devotee is sthiramatiḥ — one whose understanding is firm. This is not temporary inspiration or emotional devotion alone. The mind is steady because the knowledge of the Self is firm. Such a bhaktimān naraḥ — such a devoted person — is dear to Bhagavān.
key_verses.story:
Sadāśiva Brahmendra was a great saint known for his deep absorption in the Self.
He wandered freely, often unaware of the outer world. He did not live by social expectations, possessions, or fixed identity. His life itself seemed to say that the Self is not confined to a house, role, or public image.
One day, while absorbed inwardly, he wandered into a royal camp. The guards and attendants were startled. They did not understand his state. To them, he looked like someone who had violated ordinary boundaries and customs.
The ruler became angry.
In some traditional accounts, Sadāśiva Brahmendra was severely punished. But what is remembered most is not the outer event; it is his inner stillness. He did not protest in anger. He did not defend his dignity. He did not curse those who misunderstood him.
He remained absorbed.
Later, when the ruler realized that this was no ordinary wandering man, but a great saint, remorse arose. Reverence replaced anger.
For Sadāśiva Brahmendra, neither insult nor later reverence seems to have altered his inner state. When he was misunderstood, he was silent. When he was honored, he remained silent. Praise and blame passed like wind across the sky.
This story beautifully reflects tulya-nindā-stutiḥ, maunī, aniketaḥ, and sthiramatiḥ. His silence was not weakness; it arose from fullness. His homelessness was not poverty; it arose from freedom. His steadiness was not dullness; it arose from firm knowledge.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Today, praise and criticism control many minds. A compliment can make us feel important; one negative comment can disturb us for hours. Social media has made this even stronger. Likes, shares, ratings, reviews, and public approval easily become emotional food.
This verse asks us to grow out of that dependence. We may learn from feedback, but we need not live by praise or die by criticism.
The teaching on speech is equally important. Much disturbance in life comes from unnecessary speech — gossip, reaction, exaggeration, sarcasm, complaint, and impulsive messages. Maunī reminds us that spiritual maturity includes speech discipline. Silence is not only not speaking; it is also knowing when, how, and why to speak.
Contentment with whatever comes is also very relevant. We often say, “I can be peaceful only if the house, job, family, health, and finances are exactly as I want.” This verse points to a deeper peace that does not wait for perfect conditions.
Aniketaḥ is powerful for modern life. We may own a home, use comforts, and care for family, but we need not make them our identity. Bhagavān gives; Bhagavān takes; what comes is received, what goes is returned.
key_verses.reflection:
How much does my peace depend on praise, approval, and being seen positively by others?
Can I speak less impulsively and receive life more as prasāda?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Sometimes people praise us and say, ‘Very good!’ Sometimes people criticize us and say, ‘This is not good.’
Kṛṣṇa is saying that we should not become too proud when praised or too sad when criticized.
He also says we should speak carefully. We do not have to say everything that comes into our mind. A good devotee speaks kindly, speaks only what is useful, and stays happy with what is available.”
kids_content.story:
A child brought lunch to school.
One friend said, “Your lunch looks so tasty!”
The child felt happy.
Another child said, “I don’t like that food.”
The child started feeling bad.
Then the teacher said, “Pause. One person praised it. One person did not. But your lunch is still your lunch. Eat with gratitude.”
The child smiled and ate peacefully.
Later, during class, the child wanted to say something unkind about another student. But then the child remembered: “I should speak only what is kind and useful.”
So the child stayed quiet.
That day, the child learned two things: do not become too excited by praise or too sad by criticism, and speak carefully.
kids_content.reflection:
What happens in your mind when someone praises you?
Can you pause before saying something unkind?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: Sameness in Criticism and Praise
Phrase: tulya-nindā-stutiḥ = one for whom criticism and praise are equal
The mature devotee does not become inflated by praise or wounded by criticism. Praise and blame may be useful as information, but they do not define the person’s inner worth.
Step 2: Measured Speech
Phrase: maunī = one of measured speech
Maunī here means a person of limited, disciplined, thoughtful speech. It is not necessarily absolute silence. The devotee avoids unnecessary, impulsive, and harmful speech.
Step 3: Contentment with Whatever Comes
Phrase: santuṣṭo yena kenacit = content with whatever comes
The devotee is content with what comes by Bhagavān’s order. This does not prevent effort, but it prevents complaint, dependence, and emotional agitation over external arrangements.
Step 4: Freedom from Attachment to Residence
Phrase: aniketaḥ = not attached to any fixed abode
This does not merely mean physically homeless. The deeper point is freedom from possessiveness and psychological dependence on house, place, and external security. The person may live somewhere, but does not bind the mind with “this alone is mine.”
Step 5: Firm Understanding
Phrase: sthiramatiḥ = one whose understanding is firm
The devotee has firm knowledge of the Self. The mind is steady because the intellect is clear. This is not casual devotion, but mature devotion supported by steady knowledge.
Step 6: Dear to Bhagavān
Phrase: bhaktimānme priyo naraḥ = such a devoted person is dear to Me
The devotee described here has devotion, restraint, contentment, inner freedom, and firm knowledge. Such a person is dear to Bhagavān because there is no inner dependence on praise, possession, speech, or security.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Dattātreya and King Yadu
In the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, King Yadu once saw an unusual wandering sage.
The sage looked free, joyful, and untouched by the ordinary burdens of life. He had no royal protection, no fixed palace, no visible possessions, and no anxious planning. Yet he seemed inwardly full.
Yadu was puzzled.
“How can you be so happy,” he asked, “when you move through the world without attachment?”
The sage was Dattātreya.
Dattātreya explained that he had learned from the whole world. Earth taught patience. Air taught freedom from attachment. Sky taught vastness. Water taught purity. Fire taught brightness and detachment. Many beings and situations became his teachers.
He was not bound to one place or one arrangement. Wherever he went, life taught him. Whatever came, he received. Whatever went, he allowed to go.
This is aniketaḥ and santuṣṭo yena kenacit. The world itself became his āśrama, because his mind was not imprisoned by possession.
His contentment did not come from having everything. It came from needing very little and learning from everything.
2. Story of Paṭṭinattār and the Needle
Paṭṭinattār was once a wealthy merchant. His ships brought goods, his house was full, and society respected him.
Then one day, a small incident shattered his old understanding.
A message came to him connected with a simple needle — a reminder that not even a needle without an eye can be taken after death. The meaning struck him deeply. All the wealth he had gathered, all the status he had enjoyed, all the security he had built — none of it could be carried beyond death.
He did not merely feel sad for a day and return to business.
He renounced.
Leaving behind wealth, home, and status, he became a wandering saint. People who once honored him may have thought him mad. Some praised him as a saint; others mocked him as one who had lost everything.
But Paṭṭinattār had seen through the game.
His home was no longer his identity. Praise and blame no longer had the old hold. He became aniketaḥ — free from dependence on residence and possession — and sthiramatiḥ, firm in the clarity that the Self is not enriched by wealth nor diminished by poverty.
3. Story of Māṇikkavācakar and the King’s Horses
Māṇikkavācakar served as a minister to the Pāṇḍya king. Once he was entrusted with royal wealth to buy horses for the kingdom.
On the way, he encountered the presence of Bhagavān Śiva in a transforming way. His heart was seized by devotion. Instead of buying horses, he used the wealth for building and serving a temple.
When the king heard this, anger rose. To the king, it looked like betrayal. Māṇikkavācakar was summoned and accused.
Praise turned into blame. Honor turned into humiliation.
But Māṇikkavācakar remained absorbed in Bhagavān. He did not defend himself with panic. He did not become broken by public dishonor.
The story continues with Bhagavān Śiva intervening in a wondrous way, showing that what appeared as worldly failure was in truth divine play.
Māṇikkavācakar’s steadiness shows tulya-nindā-stutiḥ. When honored as minister, he did not lose himself. When blamed as a failure, he did not lose himself. His mind was with Bhagavān.
4. Story of Kāraikkāl Ammaiyār Leaving Beauty and Home
Kāraikkāl Ammaiyār lived as a devoted woman in a household. One day, a mango came into her home, and through a series of events connected with devotion to Bhagavān Śiva, her husband realized that she was not an ordinary person.
Instead of seeing her as a wife alone, he began to revere her as divine.
For Kāraikkāl Ammaiyār, this became a turning point. She did not cling to household identity, beauty, marriage-status, or social role. Her heart belonged to Bhagavān Śiva.
She prayed to be freed from ordinary bodily beauty so that no worldly identity would bind her. She left the conventional life and became one of the great devotees of Śiva.
Her story is not about rejection born of bitterness. It is about freedom from identity and possession.
She became aniketā in spirit — not bound by house, body, beauty, or social definition. Praise and blame from the world could not measure her life. Her mind was firm in Bhagavān.
5. Story of Ṛṣabhadeva as an Avadhūta
In the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, Ṛṣabhadeva is described as one who had already fulfilled his responsibilities and then moved through the world as an avadhūta.
He had been a king, a teacher, and a guide. But after instructing his sons, he gave up royal life and wandered freely, unattached to residence, possession, status, and public image.
People did not always understand him.
Some mocked him. Some insulted him. Some treated him as though he were mad. To ordinary eyes, a king who had given up everything and moved without concern for appearance seemed strange.
But Ṛṣabhadeva remained untouched.
Praise did not attract him. Insult did not disturb him. He did not speak to defend his status. He did not try to prove his greatness. His silence was not helplessness; it came from inner fullness.
He had no fixed dependence on home, role, or recognition. The world could call him noble or mad, king or wanderer — none of these labels altered his understanding.
This story beautifully reflects tulya-nindā-stutiḥ, maunī, aniketaḥ, and sthiramatiḥ. One who is established inwardly does not need the world’s praise and is not injured by the world’s criticism.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः मौनी सन्तुष्टः येन केनचित् ।
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिः भक्तिमान् मे प्रियः नरः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः = one for whom criticism and praise are equal | मौनी = one of measured speech / silent one | सन्तुष्टः = contented | येन केनचित् = with anything whatsoever | अनिकेतः = homeless / not attached to residence | स्थिरमतिः = one whose understanding is firm | भक्तिमान् = endowed with devotion | मे = to Me / for Me | प्रियः = dear | नरः = person
rich_grammar.anvayah: यः नरः तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः, मौनी, येन केनचित् सन्तुष्टः, अनिकेतः, स्थिरमतिः, भक्तिमान्, सः मे प्रियः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: निन्दा + स्तुतिः = निन्दास्तुतिः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final आ remains before स् without change. | तुल्य + निन्दास्तुतिः = तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final अ joins with न् without special phonetic change. | तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः + मौनी = तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga after इ changes to र् before the soft consonant म्. | सन्तुष्टः + येन = सन्तुष्टो येन | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final अः becomes ओ before the semivowel य्. | स्थिरमतिः + भक्तिमान् = स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान् | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga after इ changes to र् before the soft consonant भ्. | भक्तिमान् + मे = भक्तिमान्मे | Sandhi name: संयोगसन्धिः; final न् joins with the following म् in continuous pronunciation. | मे + प्रियः = मे प्रियः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; no special phonetic change. | प्रियः + नरः = प्रियो नरः | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final अः becomes ओ before the soft consonant न्.
rich_grammar.samasa: तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिः = बहुव्रीहि समासः with द्वन्द्व inside | Breakdown: निन्दा च स्तुतिः च = निन्दास्तुती; तुल्ये निन्दास्तुती यस्य सः — one for whom criticism and praise are equal. | अनिकेतः = नञ्-तत्पुरुष / बहुव्रीहि sense | Breakdown: न केतः / न निश्चितं केतनं यस्य सः — one who has no fixed dependence on residence. | स्थिरमतिः = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: स्थिरा मतिः यस्य सः — one whose understanding is firm.
rich_grammar.other: सन्तुष्टः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from सम्-तुष् धातुः, meaning “contented.” | मौनी is formed from मौन with इनि/इन् sense, meaning “one endowed with silence” or “one of restrained speech.” | भक्तिमान् is formed with मतुप्-प्रत्ययः, meaning “one who possesses bhakti.” | येन केनचित् is an indefinite expression in तृतीया-विभक्ति, meaning “with anything whatsoever.” | All major descriptive terms are प्रथमा-एकवचनम् and qualify नरः.

key_verses.final_takeaway: Equal in criticism and praise, measured in speech, content with whatever comes, free from attachment to home and security, and firm in Self-knowledge — such a devoted person is dear to Bhagavān.

key_verses.title: The Immortal Dharma
key_verses.sanskrit:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं(य्ँ) यथोक्तं(म्) पर्युपासते ।
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा(:) भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः ॥
key_verses.transliteration:
ye tu dharmyāmṛtamidaṁ(y̐) yathoktaṁ(m) paryupāsate ।
śraddadhānā matparamā(ḥ) bhaktāste'tīva me priyāḥ ॥
key_verses.one_line_meaning: Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa concludes that those devotees who, with śraddhā and with Bhagavān as the supreme goal, sincerely follow this immortal dharma as taught are exceedingly dear to Him.
key_verses.concise_journey: This verse is the concluding verse of the twelfth chapter. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa has already taught the full range of bhakti-yoga: karma-yoga, upāsanā-yoga, and jñāna-yoga. He has also described the qualities of the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gone through these disciplines and gained inner freedom.
key_verses.meaning_detail:
This verse is the concluding verse of the twelfth chapter. Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa has already taught the full range of bhakti-yoga: karma-yoga, upāsanā-yoga, and jñāna-yoga. He has also described the qualities of the parā-bhakta, the mature devotee who has gone through these disciplines and gained inner freedom.
Now He gathers the whole teaching and calls it dharmyāmṛtam — the immortal dharma. It is dharmya because it is a righteous, valid, scriptural way of life. It is amṛtam because it leads to immortality, meaning freedom from saṁsāra. This is not merely a list of good manners; it is the way of life that culminates in mokṣa.
The phrase idaṁ yathoktam means “this, as taught.” Bhagavān is referring to the teaching already unfolded: the stages of bhakti-yoga and the qualities of the parā-bhakta. These qualities are not independent moral decorations. They are the natural expression of a mind that has gone through karma-yoga, upāsanā, and jñāna.
Those who follow this teaching are śraddadhānāḥ — endowed with śraddhā. Śraddhā is not blind belief. It is trust in the śāstra and the guru long enough to live the teaching and discover its truth. They are also matparamāḥ — those for whom Bhagavān is the supreme goal. Bhagavān is not one interest among many; Bhagavān is the final end of life.
Such devotees are atīva me priyāḥ — exceedingly dear to Bhagavān. This is the final statement of the chapter: the dearest devotee is the one who receives the teaching with śraddhā, lives it, matures through it, and comes to abide in Bhagavān as the highest truth.
key_verses.story:
In the Rāmāyaṇa, the life of Bharata beautifully reflects the spirit of living a teaching as dharma, with Bhagavān as the supreme goal.
When Bharata returned to Ayodhyā and discovered what had happened, he was shaken. Rāma had been sent to the forest, Daśaratha had died in grief, and the kingdom had come to Bharata because of Kaikeyī’s demand.
Bharata could have accepted the throne. The ministers, citizens, and royal machinery were all available. He had the outer opportunity to rule.
But his heart did not move toward power.
He went to the forest and begged Rāma to return. For Bharata, Rāma was not merely an elder brother; Rāma was dharma itself. If Rāma would not return because of His commitment to Daśaratha’s word, Bharata would not violate that dharma either.
So he took Rāma’s pādukās, placed them on the throne, and ruled only as a caretaker.
This was not symbolic for one day. For fourteen years, Bharata lived with restraint. He did not enjoy the kingdom as personal possession. He waited for Rāma. Every act of administration was done in Rāma’s name. His life was governed by śraddhā, dharma, and commitment to Bhagavān Rāma as the supreme center.
This is dharmyāmṛtam paryupāsate in living form. Bharata did not merely admire dharma; he lived it. He did not merely love Rāma emotionally; he made Rāma the highest reference point for life.
Such devotion is not casual affection. It is a life shaped by Bhagavān.
key_verses.real_life_example:
Many people appreciate spiritual teachings, but appreciation alone does not transform life. We may like the Gītā, quote verses, admire saints, and enjoy spiritual ideas. But the question is: do we live the teaching?
This verse asks us to move from admiration to assimilation. Yathoktam paryupāsate means we take the teaching as it has been given and stay with it. We do not merely pick what is emotionally pleasing and ignore what challenges our ego.
In modern life, śraddhā is difficult because we are trained to be impatient. We want quick results. We want to test everything immediately by personal comfort. But deep transformation requires trust, time, and steady practice.
The phrase matparamāḥ is also very important. We may say spirituality matters, but often career, money, image, comfort, family approval, or personal success become the real supreme goals. This verse invites us to gently examine what actually governs our choices.
When Bhagavān becomes the highest goal, life becomes reorganized. Work remains, family remains, responsibilities remain, but they are no longer disconnected from spiritual growth. Everything becomes part of the journey toward freedom.
key_verses.reflection:
Do I merely admire the teaching, or am I trying to live it as a dharma?
What is truly parama for me — Bhagavān and mokṣa, or some temporary worldly goal?

kids_content.explanation_script:
“Suppose your teacher gives you good advice: be kind, tell the truth, do your work carefully, and do not hurt others.
It is not enough to say, ‘That is good advice.’ We have to try to live it.
Kṛṣṇa is saying that the people who trust His teaching and try to live it with love are very dear to Him. They do not just listen; they practice.”
kids_content.story:
A child’s teacher said, “Every day, do one kind thing without asking for praise.”
The child liked the idea.
The next day, the child helped a classmate pick up fallen books. No one saw it.
At first, the child thought, “No one praised me.”
Then the child remembered, “Teacher said to do kindness because it is right, not because someone claps.”
So the child smiled quietly.
Day after day, the child tried to follow the teaching. Sometimes it was easy. Sometimes the child forgot. But the child kept trying.
Slowly, kindness became natural.
That is what Kṛṣṇa is teaching. A good devotee listens with trust and then lives the teaching.
kids_content.reflection:
What is one good teaching you can practice today?
Can you do something right even if no one praises you?

key_verses.detailed_meaning:
Step 1: The Devotees Being Addressed
Phrase: ye tu = but those who
Bhagavān Kṛṣṇa now speaks of those devotees who receive and live the teaching that has been unfolded in this chapter. This is the concluding statement.
Step 2: The Teaching as Immortal Dharma
Phrase: dharmyāmṛtam idaṁ = this immortal dharma
The teaching is dharmya because it is righteous, scriptural, and aligned with spiritual growth. It is amṛtam because it leads to immortality — freedom from saṁsāra. The qualities described are not ordinary social virtues alone; they belong to the path that culminates in mokṣa.
Step 3: As Taught
Phrase: yathoktam = as taught
The teaching is to be understood as it has been unfolded. It includes the full bhakti-yoga ladder and the characteristics of the parā-bhakta. We should not distort the teaching according to convenience.
Step 4: Living the Teaching
Phrase: paryupāsate = they worship / follow / live with reverence
Here the sense is not merely ritual worship. It means reverentially embracing and practicing this teaching. The devotee lives in alignment with this dharma.
Step 5: Śraddhā
Phrase: śraddadhānāḥ = endowed with śraddhā
Śraddhā is trust in the teaching and the teacher. It allows us to stay with the discipline even before the full result is visible. Without śraddhā, the mind will keep doubting, dropping, and changing direction.
Step 6: Bhagavān as Supreme Goal
Phrase: matparamāḥ = having Me as the supreme goal
For these devotees, Bhagavān is the highest end. Other goals may exist at the transactional level, but they are not ultimate. The life-direction is toward Bhagavān and mokṣa.
Step 7: Exceedingly Dear
Phrase: bhaktāste'tīva me priyāḥ = those devotees are exceedingly dear to Me
Bhagavān concludes with the highest praise. Those who live this immortal dharma with śraddhā and with Bhagavān as the supreme goal are not merely dear; they are exceedingly dear.
key_verses.more_stories:
1. Story of Bharata and the Pādukās
When Bharata returned to Ayodhyā, he did not find the joy of a coronation. Instead, he found grief, confusion, and moral shock.
Rāma had gone to the forest. Daśaratha was gone. The throne stood empty, and the kingdom was waiting.
Everyone looked to Bharata.
He could have said, “This has come to me by destiny.” He could have sat on the throne and ruled as king. Many would have accepted it.
But Bharata’s heart would not allow it.
He went to the forest and fell at Rāma’s feet, begging Him to return. Rāma refused, because Daśaratha’s word had to be protected.
Then Bharata did something extraordinary. He took Rāma’s pādukās back to Ayodhyā, placed them on the throne, and ruled as servant, not owner.
For fourteen years, he lived with one reference point: Rāma. His life was not guided by ambition, comfort, or personal identity.
This story shows śraddadhānaḥ and matparamaḥ. Bharata trusted dharma and made Bhagavān Rāma the supreme goal of his life.
2. Story of Śabala’s Devotion to Vasiṣṭha
In the Rāmāyaṇa tradition, Viśvāmitra once visited the āśrama of Vasiṣṭha. There he saw the divine cow Śabala, who could provide all that was needed for hospitality.
Viśvāmitra desired Śabala and asked Vasiṣṭha to give her to him. When Vasiṣṭha refused, the king tried to take her by force.
Śabala was pulled away, but her heart remained with Vasiṣṭha.
She cried out to her master. She did not think, “The king is powerful, so I should follow power.” Her devotion and loyalty were not based on fear or convenience.
Vasiṣṭha told her that if she wished, she could resist. From her arose great strength, and Viśvāmitra’s force was overcome.
This story shows the power of fidelity to dharma and guru. Śabala’s loyalty was not sentimental weakness. It was unwavering alignment with the rightful center.
In 12.20, the devotee is matparamaḥ — one for whom Bhagavān is supreme. Śabala’s story gives us a simple image: when the heart knows its true refuge, it does not run after power.
3. Story of Upamanyu and the Guru’s Instruction
Upamanyu was a young student under the guidance of his guru. His teacher tested him repeatedly.
When food was restricted, Upamanyu tried different ways to sustain himself. Each time, the guru corrected him. Finally, through hardship and mistake, Upamanyu suffered and fell into a well.
In that helpless condition, he did not abandon trust in the guru or the sacred path. He prayed, and by divine grace he was restored.
When he returned, the guru was pleased with his endurance, faith, and obedience.
The story is not about blind suffering. It is about śraddhā — the willingness to stay with a discipline until its deeper purpose is understood.
In spiritual life, we often want teachings that are immediately pleasant. But dharmyāmṛtam must be followed with patience. Upamanyu’s story shows how śraddhā carries a seeker through difficulty.
4. Story of Sāvitrī Following Dharma
Sāvitrī chose Satyavān as her husband, even though she was warned that his life would be short.
She did not choose based on convenience. Once she had recognized dharma, she stood by it with clarity.
When the destined day came, Satyavān fell in the forest. Yama came to take his life. Sāvitrī followed Yama with steadiness, humility, intelligence, and devotion.
Yama asked her to turn back, but she continued, speaking words of dharma with great composure.
She did not argue wildly. She did not collapse in grief. She did not abandon her commitment.
Moved by her wisdom and fidelity, Yama granted boons, and finally Satyavān’s life was restored.
Sāvitrī’s story shows what it means to live dharma with śraddhā. She did not merely talk about dharma; she followed it through fear, grief, and uncertainty.
5. Story of Pothana Offering the Bhāgavatam to Rāma
Pothana was a poet and devotee who composed the Telugu Bhāgavatam. Such a work could have brought fame, patronage, and royal approval.
A ruler desired that the work be dedicated to him.
But Pothana’s heart was clear. The Bhāgavatam was not written for worldly praise. It belonged to Bhagavān.
He offered it to Bhagavān Rāma.
This was not stubbornness for personal pride. It was matparamatvam. Bhagavān was the supreme recipient, not a worldly patron.
Through this act, Pothana showed that even literary talent, learning, and artistic beauty become sacred when offered to Bhagavān.
This story beautifully reflects 12.20. The devotee with śraddhā follows the immortal dharma and keeps Bhagavān as the highest goal.

rich_grammar.padacchedah:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतम् इदम् यथोक्तम् पर्युपासते ।
श्रद्दधानाः मत्परमाः भक्ताः ते अतीव मे प्रियाः ॥
rich_grammar.pratipadarthah: ये = those who | तु = but / indeed | धर्म्यामृतम् = the immortal dharma / dharma leading to immortality | इदम् = this | यथोक्तम् = as taught / as stated | पर्युपासते = worship / follow with reverence | श्रद्दधानाः = endowed with śraddhā | मत्परमाः = having Me as the supreme goal | भक्ताः = devotees | ते = they | अतीव = exceedingly | मे = to Me / for Me | प्रियाः = dear
rich_grammar.anvayah: ये तु श्रद्दधानाः मत्परमाः भक्ताः इदम् यथोक्तम् धर्म्यामृतम् पर्युपासते, ते मे अतीव प्रियाः ।
rich_grammar.sandhi: धर्म्य + अमृतम् = धर्म्यामृतम् | Sandhi name: सवर्णदीर्घसन्धिः; final अ and initial अ combine into आ. | धर्म्यामृतम् + इदम् = धर्म्यामृतमिदम् | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; final म् joins directly with the following vowel इ without visible change. | यथा + उक्तम् = यथोक्तम् | Sandhi name: गुणसन्धिः; final आ with initial उ gives ओ in the combined form. | भक्ताः + ते = भक्तास्ते | Sandhi name: विसर्गसन्धिः; final visarga becomes स् before the dental consonant त्. | ते + अतीव = तेऽतीव | Sandhi name: पूर्वरूपसन्धिः; final ए remains and the following अ is elided, marked by अवग्रहः. | अतीव + मे = अतीव मे | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; no special phonetic change. | मे + प्रियाः = मे प्रियाः | Sandhi name: संहितायाम् पदसन्धिः; no special phonetic change.
rich_grammar.samasa: धर्म्यामृतम् = कर्मधारय / तत्पुरुष sense | Breakdown: धर्म्यम् अमृतम् — the righteous teaching that is immortal / the dharma that leads to immortality. | यथोक्तम् = अव्ययीभाव समासः | Breakdown: यथा उक्तम् — as stated / as taught. | मत्परमाः = बहुव्रीहि समासः | Breakdown: अहं परमः येषां ते — those for whom I am the supreme goal.
rich_grammar.other: पर्युपासते is लट्-लकारः, प्रथमपुरुषः, बहुवचनम्, आत्मनेपदम् from परि-उप-आस् धातुः. | श्रद्दधानाः is a शानच्-प्रत्ययान्त form, meaning “having śraddhā” or “endowed with trust.” | उक्तम् in यथोक्तम् is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त-कृदन्तम् from वच् धातुः. | भक्ताः is a क्तप्रत्ययान्त form from भज् धातुः. | अतीव is an अव्ययम् intensifying प्रियाः, meaning “exceedingly dear.”

key_verses.final_takeaway: Those who follow this immortal dharma with śraddhā and keep Bhagavān as the supreme goal are exceedingly dear to Him. The teaching must not merely be admired but lived — that is the essence of true devotion.
